source: lecture: Operations / 3. Compliance with Regulatory Bodies

front.question: What changes did the HITECH Act (part of ARRA 2009) introduce to HIPAA?

back.question: What changes did the HITECH Act (part of ARRA 2009) introduce to HIPAA?

options.A: It created the 18 PHI identifiers and required covered entities to encrypt all data
options.B: It extended HIPAA Security Rule compliance to business associates, added breach notification requirements, and gave patients the right to electronic access of their records
options.C: It replaced the original HIPAA privacy rule with new federal standards for telemedicine
options.D: It limited HIPAA applicability to hospitals with more than 100 beds

back.explanation: The HITECH Act (via ARRA 2009) made business associates (vendors, contractors) directly subject to HIPAA Security Rule; required notification when unsecured PHI is breached; and gave patients the right to electronic access of their records (patient portals, electronic copies on CD). It also introduced criminal penalties and increased monetary fines.

source: lecture: Operations / 3. Compliance with Regulatory Bodies

front.question: MIPPA primarily impacts physician practices by requiring accreditation on which imaging equipment to receive CMS reimbursement?

back.question: MIPPA primarily impacts physician practices by requiring accreditation on which imaging equipment to receive CMS reimbursement?

options.A: X-ray and fluoroscopy equipment
options.B: Ultrasound and mammography equipment
options.C: MRI, CT, PET, and nuclear medicine equipment
options.D: All imaging equipment including plain radiography

back.explanation: MIPPA (Medicare Improvements for Patients and Providers Act) requires accreditation on MRI, CT, PET, and nuclear medicine (NM) equipment in physician practices to receive CMS reimbursement. It also added quality reporting initiatives, personnel qualification requirements, and random on-site visits.

front.question: Under MQSA, how often must mammography equipment be surveyed by a qualified physicist?

back.question: Under MQSA, how often must mammography equipment be surveyed by a qualified physicist?

options.A: Every two years and after any software upgrade
options.B: Upon installation, annually, and whenever major parts are replaced
options.C: Monthly for the first year, then annually thereafter
options.D: Only upon initial installation and after reported equipment failures

back.explanation: MQSA requires mammography equipment to be individually surveyed by qualified physicists upon installation, annually, and whenever major parts are replaced. Facilities must also store weekly phantom images and monitor QA minimum specifications.

source: lecture: Operations / 3. Compliance with Regulatory Bodies

front.question: Under MQSA, what format must mammography images be stored in?

back.question: Under MQSA, what format must mammography images be stored in?

options.A: Lossy compressed format to reduce storage costs
options.B: Any DICOM-compliant format at the facility's discretion
options.C: Uncompressed or lossless compressed format
options.D: JPEG format with a minimum quality factor of 85

back.explanation: MQSA requires mammography images to be stored in uncompressed or lossless compressed format to preserve image quality for accurate diagnosis and comparison over time. Lossy compression is explicitly prohibited for mammography.

front.question: A BI-RADS category 4 mammography finding means what?

back.question: A BI-RADS category 4 mammography finding means what?

options.A: Negative — no abnormality found
options.B: Probably benign — short-term follow-up recommended
options.C: Suspicious abnormality
options.D: Known biopsy-proven malignancy

back.explanation: BI-RADS scale: 0=incomplete, 1=negative, 2=benign, 3=probably benign, 4=suspicious abnormality, 5=highly suggestive of malignancy, 6=known biopsy-proven malignancy. Category 4 warrants biopsy consideration but is not definitive for malignancy.

front.question: Which of the following is a responsibility of the American College of Radiology (ACR)?

back.question: Which of the following is a responsibility of the American College of Radiology (ACR)?

options.A: Setting Medicare reimbursement rates for radiology procedures
options.B: Serving as the MQSA accrediting agency and the CMS accrediting agency for MRI, CT, NM, and PET
options.C: Mandating annual physics surveys for all radiology equipment in physician offices
options.D: Establishing criminal penalties for HIPAA violations by covered entities

back.explanation: The ACR serves as the MQSA accrediting agency (for mammography) and as the CMS accrediting agency for MRI, CT, NM, and PET. It also develops practice guidelines for appropriate imaging, sets standards for display devices, provides teleradiology guidelines, and accredits Breast Centers of Excellence.

front.question: Which accrediting body, other than the ACR, is widely recognized across all healthcare organizations (not just imaging) for accreditation and certification?

back.question: Which accrediting body, other than the ACR, is widely recognized across all healthcare organizations (not just imaging) for accreditation and certification?

options.A: IAC (Intersocietal Accreditation Commission)
options.B: HIMSS
options.C: Joint Commission (JC)
options.D: RSNA

back.explanation: The Joint Commission is a widely recognized accrediting body for hospitals and healthcare organizations broadly. The IAC accredits imaging facilities specifically. HIMSS and RSNA are professional societies, not accrediting bodies.

source: lecture: Operations / 3. Compliance with Regulatory Bodies

front.question: A state's privacy law requires patient data to be retained for 12 years, while the federal HIPAA standard requires only 6 years. Which applies?

back.question: A state's privacy law requires patient data to be retained for 12 years, while the federal HIPAA standard requires only 6 years. Which applies?

options.A: The federal HIPAA standard always supersedes state law
options.B: The organization can choose whichever standard is more convenient
options.C: The stricter state law applies, because state laws may be stricter than federal law
options.D: Neither applies until CMS issues a ruling specific to that state

back.explanation: Compliance levels range from federal to state to regional/local to organizational. A critical rule is that state laws may be stricter than federal regulations, and when they are, the stricter law applies. The organization must comply with the 12-year state requirement.

front.question: Which of the following is an IIP's responsibility when working with the HIPAA compliance officer?

back.question: Which of the following is an IIP's responsibility when working with the HIPAA compliance officer?

options.A: Writing the organization's privacy policy and filing it with the Department of Health and Human Services
options.B: Setting auto-logoff on RIS and PACS systems, performing random audits of information access, and reviewing orphaned user logins
options.C: Issuing criminal penalties to employees who access records without authorization
options.D: Performing annual financial audits of the organization's HIPAA compliance program

back.explanation: IIPs work with the HIPAA compliance officer by: issuing logins per compliance officer policies, performing random audits, terminating logins upon user termination/leave, setting auto-logoff on RIS and PACS, and reviewing orphaned user accounts. Criminal enforcement is a government function.

front.question: Which type of hospital partners with medical and nursing schools and offers education and research programs?

back.question: Which type of hospital partners with medical and nursing schools and offers education and research programs?

options.A: General hospital
options.B: Specialty hospital
options.C: Teaching hospital
options.D: Research hospital

back.explanation: Teaching hospitals partner with medical/nursing schools and offer education and research programs. Research hospitals provide care for complex conditions and move research results into practice. Specialty hospitals focus on particular organ systems or specific diseases.

source: lecture: Communications / 1. Roles and Relationships in the Healthcare Setting

front.question: What is the primary purpose of medical terminology in healthcare?

back.question: What is the primary purpose of medical terminology in healthcare?

options.A: To restrict access to clinical information to credentialed professionals only
options.B: To create a standard common language for healthcare professionals to communicate effectively and efficiently
options.C: To satisfy billing requirements for CPT and ICD coding
options.D: To ensure regulatory compliance with HIPAA identifiers

back.explanation: Medical terminology creates a standard way for healthcare professionals to communicate effectively and efficiently using a common language. This is critical for patient-centric care as digital imaging expands across specialties beyond radiology and cardiology.

front.question: An axial (transverse) image plane divides the body in which way?

back.question: An axial (transverse) image plane divides the body in which way?

options.A: Vertically into anterior and posterior parts
options.B: Vertically into right and left parts
options.C: Horizontally into upper and lower parts
options.D: Diagonally through the midline from head to foot

back.explanation: The axial (transverse) plane is horizontal, dividing the body into upper and lower parts. The coronal (frontal) plane is vertical, dividing into anterior and posterior. The sagittal plane is vertical, dividing into right and left.

front.question: Which imaging modality uses non-ionizing radiation in the form of a magnetic field and radiofrequency pulses?

back.question: Which imaging modality uses non-ionizing radiation in the form of a magnetic field and radiofrequency pulses?

options.A: CT (Computed Tomography)
options.B: Nuclear Medicine (NM/PET)
options.C: MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging)
options.D: Fluoroscopy (RF)

back.explanation: MRI uses non-ionizing radiation — specifically a magnetic field and radiofrequency (RF) pulses to acquire images using protons in water and fat. CT, fluoroscopy, mammography, and nuclear medicine all use ionizing radiation (X-ray or radionuclides). Ultrasound uses high-frequency sound waves.

front.question: ICD codes are maintained by which organization and what is their primary purpose?

back.question: ICD codes are maintained by which organization and what is their primary purpose?

options.A: AMA — to describe treatment services rendered by a hospital
options.B: RSNA — to standardize radiology reporting terminology
options.C: CDC's National Center for Health Statistics under WHO authorization — to classify diseases and describe why a patient is seeking care
options.D: Regenstreif Institute — to identify measurements and observations

back.explanation: ICD (International Classification of Diseases) is maintained by CDC's NCHS under WHO authorization. It classifies diseases and describes why a patient is seeking care (diagnosis), is used for billing and statistics, and encodes cause of death. ICD-11 took effect January 2022.

front.question: What is the key functional difference between CPT codes and ICD codes?

back.question: What is the key functional difference between CPT codes and ICD codes?

options.A: CPT codes are used internationally; ICD codes are used only in the US
options.B: CPT codes describe the treatment services provided; ICD codes describe why the patient needed those services (diagnosis)
options.C: CPT codes are maintained by RSNA; ICD codes are maintained by the AMA
options.D: CPT codes are for inpatient billing; ICD codes are for outpatient billing

back.explanation: CPT (Current Procedural Terminology) codes describe what services were rendered (maintained by AMA's CPT Editorial Panel). ICD codes describe the reason the patient sought care — the diagnosis or disease classification. The two are tied together: CPT for service, ICD for diagnosis.

front.question: RADLEX is maintained by which organization and what is its primary purpose?

back.question: RADLEX is maintained by which organization and what is its primary purpose?

options.A: AMA — to set reimbursement rates for radiology procedures
options.B: RSNA — controlled radiology terminology to improve report clarity, standardize key elements, improve speech recognition, and reduce radiologist variation
options.C: Regenstreif Institute — universal codes for lab measurements and observations
options.D: SNOMED International — comprehensive multilingual clinical terminology

back.explanation: RADLEX (Radiology Lexicon) is maintained by RSNA. It provides controlled terminology for radiology designed to improve clarity of radiology reports, standardize key elements for improved speech recognition and PACS display layouts, and reduce radiologist variation.

front.question: Which type of change management request does NOT require Change Advisory Board (CAB) approval?

back.question: Which type of change management request does NOT require Change Advisory Board (CAB) approval?

options.A: Normal change
options.B: Emergency change
options.C: Standard change
options.D: Major change

back.explanation: Standard changes are low-risk, periodical changes that do not require CAB approval. Normal changes (major or minor) require CAB approval. Emergency changes require immediate approval but through a different process given urgency. There is no official 'major change' category.

front.question: What is the correct sequence of steps in the change management process?

back.question: What is the correct sequence of steps in the change management process?

options.A: Approval → Request → Planning → Implementation → Review
options.B: Request → Evaluation & Planning → Approval → Implementation → Review & Closure
options.C: Planning → Testing → Implementation → Approval → Closure
options.D: Request → CAB Review → Testing → Implementation → Approval

back.explanation: The five-step change management process is: Request (document the change) → Evaluation & Planning (scope the work) → Approval (leadership and CAB) → Implementation (carry out with stakeholder updates) → Review & Closure (validate, document, formally close).

source: lecture: Communications / 4. Change Management and System Availability

front.question: During an unscheduled PACS downtime, what is the IIP's first recommended action?

back.question: During an unscheduled PACS downtime, what is the IIP's first recommended action?

options.A: Immediately escalate to the vendor without any investigation
options.B: Assess whether the downtime is isolated to one area or system-wide, then perform troubleshooting steps before escalating
options.C: Notify all referring physicians by phone before attempting any technical resolution
options.D: Shut down all connected modalities to prevent data corruption

back.explanation: During unscheduled downtime, the IIP should first assess whether it is local or system-wide, then perform troubleshooting steps before escalating. After that: escalate appropriately, communicate end-user impact to internal teams, and correct broken studies once the system is back online.

front.question: LOINC codes are maintained by which organization and are primarily used to encode what types of data?

back.question: LOINC codes are maintained by which organization and are primarily used to encode what types of data?

options.A: RSNA — imaging procedures and body part descriptions
options.B: AMA — treatment services rendered in outpatient settings
options.C: Regenstreif Institute — measurements, observations, and documents
options.D: SNOMED International — anatomical structures and clinical findings

back.explanation: LOINC (Logical Observation Identifiers Names and Codes) is maintained by the Regenstreif Institute and endorsed by ACLA and CAP. It provides universal codes and structured names to identify things you can measure or observe, and is used to encode measurements, observations, and documents.

front.question: Which survey type yields the highest response rate and most accurate data but requires trained interviewers?

back.question: Which survey type yields the highest response rate and most accurate data but requires trained interviewers?

options.A: Email survey
options.B: Paper-based survey
options.C: Phone survey
options.D: In-person survey

back.explanation: In-person surveys have the highest response rate, yield the most accurate data and greatest data volume (sometimes extra information), but require trained interviewers. Email surveys have generally high return rates but no interviewer. Phone surveys are fast and inexpensive but must be short.

front.question: What is set induction in the context of adult education?

back.question: What is set induction in the context of adult education?

options.A: A testing method used at the end of training to measure retention
options.B: The initial act by the instructor to establish a frame of reference between the learner's experience and the objectives of the learning experience
options.C: A training style where a super-user trains their peers
options.D: A technique for organizing training materials into hierarchical outlines

back.explanation: Set induction bridges the gap between what learners already know and what they are about to learn. It focuses pupil attention on a familiar experience, creates a communicative link between experience and learning goals, and has been shown to promote gains in teaching effectiveness.

source: lecture: Education and Training / 0. Adult Education Theory

front.question: According to Knowles (1970), which of the following is a characteristic of adult learners?

back.question: According to Knowles (1970), which of the following is a characteristic of adult learners?

options.A: They rely primarily on the instructor to determine what they need to know
options.B: They prefer passive lecture-based instruction over hands-on activities
options.C: They are goal oriented, relevancy oriented, practical, and come with an accumulated foundation of experience
options.D: They are primarily motivated by grades and external rewards from supervisors

back.explanation: Knowles (1970) identified adult learner characteristics: accumulated foundation of experience and knowledge, need for respect, goal oriented, relevancy oriented, and practical. Adult learners want to know 'what's in it for me' — they need to see how the training applies to their day-to-day work.

front.question: A radiologist being trained on new PACS software immediately grabs the mouse and starts clicking around before the instructor has finished explaining. What type of learner is this?

back.question: A radiologist being trained on new PACS software immediately grabs the mouse and starts clicking around before the instructor has finished explaining. What type of learner is this?

options.A: Auditory learner
options.B: Visual learner
options.C: Kinesthetic learner
options.D: Problem-based learner

back.explanation: A kinesthetic learner learns best by getting physically involved — they like hands-on interaction and have trouble staying still for long periods. The appropriate strategies include tactile activities, multimedia, frequent breaks, and letting them interact with the application directly.

source: lecture: Education and Training / 0. Adult Education Theory

front.question: Which level of Bloom's taxonomy is being targeted when a learning objective states: 'At the end of training, the technologist should be able to operate the new RIS to process a patient order'?

back.question: Which level of Bloom's taxonomy is being targeted when a learning objective states: 'At the end of training, the technologist should be able to operate the new RIS to process a patient order'?

options.A: Remembering
options.B: Understanding
options.C: Application
options.D: Creation

back.explanation: Application (also called Apply) is when the learner can use information in their workspace — keywords include execute, implement, operate, solve, use, demonstrate. Remembering is just rote recall; Understanding is describing or classifying; Creation is producing new original work.

source: lecture: Education and Training / 0. Adult Education Theory

front.question: What is the primary risk of training end users too far in advance of a system go-live?

back.question: What is the primary risk of training end users too far in advance of a system go-live?

options.A: The vendor contract may expire before the training is complete
options.B: Regulatory bodies may require re-certification if the training was not recent
options.C: Learners will forget the material before they have the opportunity to apply it
options.D: The system may be reconfigured after training, making all training invalid

back.explanation: Training too early results in little retention by the time the system goes live. Training too late (after go-live) also causes problems. The principle is to train close enough to go-live that knowledge is retained, but not after users have already started using the system without guidance.

front.question: In the Kirkpatrick model of training assessment, what does Level 4 (Results) measure?

back.question: In the Kirkpatrick model of training assessment, what does Level 4 (Results) measure?

options.A: How learners reacted to the training experience
options.B: Whether learners retained the content of the training
options.C: Whether learners are applying what they learned on the job
options.D: Whether the training had a positive impact on the organization

back.explanation: Kirkpatrick Level 4 (Results) asks: did the training produce a positive organizational impact? (e.g., fewer errors, improved efficiency). Level 1=Reaction (learner satisfaction), Level 2=Learning (did they learn?), Level 3=Behaviour (are they using it?), Level 4=Results (organizational impact).

source: lecture: Education and Training / 2. Training Assessment

front.question: A training coordinator is setting up training for a PACS upgrade. They place staff from different roles (radiologists, technologists, registration) in the same session. Which training mistake does this represent?

back.question: A training coordinator is setting up training for a PACS upgrade. They place staff from different roles (radiologists, technologists, registration) in the same session. Which training mistake does this represent?

options.A: Training too far in advance of go-live
options.B: Trainees of differing roles in the same training session (environment mistake)
options.C: No cheat sheets or change documents provided
options.D: Training conducted in the live production system

back.explanation: Having trainees of differing roles in the same training session is categorized as an environment training mistake. Each role has different workflow needs, so mixed-role sessions lack relevance for any particular group, leading to poor retention.

front.question: Which training style involves selecting key users and teaching them deeply so they can support and train their peers?

back.question: Which training style involves selecting key users and teaching them deeply so they can support and train their peers?

options.A: Role-based training
options.B: Online training
options.C: Super-user training
options.D: Vendor training

back.explanation: Super-user training involves identifying a key person in a leadership role within a section, training them beyond standard user level, and having them serve as the expert who supports and trains others in their group. Train-the-trainer is similar but typically more formal.

source: lecture: Education and Training / 1. Training Programs

front.question: During a PACS training session, the instructor covers every menu option systematically without explaining how it relates to the technologist's daily workflow. Learners are disengaged. Which principle of effective adult training is being violated?

back.question: During a PACS training session, the instructor covers every menu option systematically without explaining how it relates to the technologist's daily workflow. Learners are disengaged. Which principle of effective adult training is being violated?

options.A: Kirkpatrick Level 3 — the instructor is not measuring behavior change
options.B: Set induction — the instructor failed to establish relevancy and bridge the learner's experience to the learning objectives
options.C: Bloom's taxonomy — the instructor targeted the wrong cognitive level
options.D: RACI diagram principles — accountability is not clearly assigned in the training

back.explanation: Set induction requires the instructor to establish a frame of reference connecting learner experience to learning goals. Adult learners are relevancy oriented — they must understand why the training matters to their work. Skipping this step leads to disengagement. The source explicitly lists 'no sense of relevancy to users' day-to-day essential workflow needs' as a trainer mistake.

front.question: In the standard radiology imaging workflow, what role does HL7 data play after an order is placed in the EMR?

back.question: In the standard radiology imaging workflow, what role does HL7 data play after an order is placed in the EMR?

options.A: HL7 data directly stores images in PACS after they are acquired
options.B: HL7 data flows from the RIS to the PACS, enabling the modality to query for DICOM Modality Worklist
options.C: HL7 data is only used after the radiologist signs the report
options.D: HL7 data is used to compress images before sending them to the modality

back.explanation: The order flows from EMR to RIS, and HL7 data then flows to PACS, allowing the modality to query for DICOM Modality Worklist (MWL). The technologist pulls up patient information from MWL to acquire images correctly. Image acquisition and storage are DICOM; order and report workflows are HL7.

source: lecture: Clinical Engineering / 0. Support for Imaging Modalities and Techniques

front.question: An AE title of 'MMC207US04' follows a standard naming convention. What does each component represent?

back.question: An AE title of 'MMC207US04' follows a standard naming convention. What does each component represent?

options.A: Manufacturer code, model number, modality type, and firmware version
options.B: Location (MMC), room number (207), modality type (US=ultrasound), and sequential device number (04)
options.C: Hospital ID, DICOM port, scan type, and study count
options.D: Network IP segment, subnet, protocol, and device ID

back.explanation: The recommended AE title naming convention is: Location | Room# | Modality type | Sequence number. MMC207US04 = location MMC, room 207, ultrasound (US), 4th device. This standardization supports troubleshooting, data mining, and communication with biomedical/clinical engineering.

source: lecture: Clinical Engineering / 0. Support for Imaging Modalities and Techniques

front.question: Mammography tomosynthesis images are significantly larger than standard 2D mammography images. What is the approximate size range for a tomosynthesis exam?

back.question: Mammography tomosynthesis images are significantly larger than standard 2D mammography images. What is the approximate size range for a tomosynthesis exam?

options.C: Average ~450 MB, up to 3 GB
options.D: Approximately 30 MB per study

back.explanation: Tomosynthesis exams consist of 40–100 stacked images, averaging ~450 MB but potentially up to 3 GB. By contrast, standard 2D mammography images are 17–27 MB each (typically 4 images). CT studies are ~30 MB. This size difference is critical for storage planning.

front.question: CT images are measured in which unit, and what is the primary benefit of this measurement?

back.question: CT images are measured in which unit, and what is the primary benefit of this measurement?

options.A: Lux — allows calibration of monitor brightness for reading rooms
options.B: Hounsfield Units (HU) — allows window leveling of images to highlight lung, bone, or soft tissue
options.C: Decibels — measures the intensity of ionizing radiation delivered to the patient
options.D: Gray — measures absorbed radiation dose for dose reporting

back.explanation: CT images are measured in Hounsfield Units (HU), which allow window leveling — adjusting brightness/contrast to highlight specific tissues such as lung, bone, or soft tissue windows. This makes radiologists more efficient and is critical for display protocol configuration in PACS.

source: lecture: Clinical Engineering / 0. Support for Imaging Modalities and Techniques

front.question: An older ultrasound machine has patient PHI burned directly onto the image pixels. Why is this a particular challenge for IIPs?

back.question: An older ultrasound machine has patient PHI burned directly onto the image pixels. Why is this a particular challenge for IIPs?

options.A: The PHI cannot be transmitted over DICOM because burned-in data exceeds the file size limit
options.B: Unlike other modalities where correcting patient data updates the DICOM header, burned-in PHI cannot be corrected by standard patient merge workflows and requires pixel-level editing
options.C: Burned-in PHI violates MQSA requirements for mammography image storage
options.D: The DICOM standard does not allow images with pixel-level PHI to be archived in PACS

back.explanation: For most modalities, patient correction workflows update the DICOM header. With older ultrasound machines that burn PHI into the image itself, the header fix alone is insufficient — the incorrect information remains visibly on the image pixels. PACS tools can block out and replace the burned text, but vendors are now enabling this feature to be toggled off.

source: lecture: Clinical Engineering / 0. Support for Imaging Modalities and Techniques

front.question: In nuclear medicine, how does the patient receive the radiopharmaceutical?

back.question: In nuclear medicine, how does the patient receive the radiopharmaceutical?

options.A: Via external radiation beam directed at the patient
options.B: Via orally, inhalation, or injection — the patient emits the radiation rather than the modality applying it
options.C: Via magnetic field resonance that activates tracer molecules already in the bloodstream
options.D: Via topical application to the skin surface for localized imaging

back.explanation: Nuclear medicine is unique because the patient receives a radiopharmaceutical (orally, inhalation, or injection) and emits the radiation — gamma rays or positrons from the radionuclide are detected by the camera. This is the opposite of X-ray/CT where the modality emits radiation through the patient.

source: lecture: Clinical Engineering / 0. Support for Imaging Modalities and Techniques

front.question: Digital pathology images are extremely large. What is the approximate compressed file size for a digital pathology image?

back.question: Digital pathology images are extremely large. What is the approximate compressed file size for a digital pathology image?

options.C: 2–3 GB (100,000 × 100,000 pixels compressed)
options.D: ~450 MB to 1 GB

back.explanation: Digital pathology images are approximately 2–3 GB at 100,000 × 100,000 pixels (compressed). This dwarfs even tomosynthesis (~450 MB–3 GB uncompressed). Organizations must plan storage accordingly before adopting digital pathology in the enterprise imaging suite.

front.question: Point-of-care (POC) ultrasound imaging in the ED or OR creates what specific data quality risk for the PACS environment?

back.question: Point-of-care (POC) ultrasound imaging in the ED or OR creates what specific data quality risk for the PACS environment?

options.A: POC ultrasound files are too large to archive in standard PACS storage
options.B: Because POC exams often lack a formal order and accession number, manually entered patient demographics create dirty data in PACS
options.C: POC ultrasound uses a different DICOM transfer syntax that is incompatible with most PACS
options.D: POC exams require radiologist interpretation, creating worklist overload

back.explanation: POC imaging is typically performed without a formal EMR order and accession number. If technologists hand-type patient demographics into the modality, the resulting data becomes 'dirty' — incorrectly or incompletely identified in PACS. IIPs should encourage placing orders for every exam, even retrospectively, to maintain data integrity.

source: lecture: Clinical Engineering / 1. Support for Ancillary Services

front.question: In DICOM, what is the role of the Service Class Provider (SCP)?

back.question: In DICOM, what is the role of the Service Class Provider (SCP)?

options.A: The SCP initiates DICOM service requests and sends image data to PACS
options.B: The SCP receives and processes DICOM service requests, performs necessary operations, and sends responses back to the SCU
options.C: The SCP compresses images before transmission to reduce bandwidth
options.D: The SCP maintains the DICOM conformance statement on the public internet

back.explanation: The SCP (Service Class Provider) receives and processes DICOM requests, performs operations, and returns responses to the SCU (Service Class User). The SCU initiates requests and sends data. Example: a modality is the SCU when it sends images; PACS is the SCP when it receives and stores them.

front.question: What DICOM service verifies that images have been successfully stored on a remote server?

back.question: What DICOM service verifies that images have been successfully stored on a remote server?

options.A: C-FIND
options.B: C-MOVE
options.C: Storage Commitment (C-STORE)
options.D: Modality Worklist (C-FIND)

back.explanation: Storage Commitment (performed via C-STORE) verifies successful storage of DICOM images on a remote server. C-FIND is used for queries (including modality worklist). C-MOVE is used for image retrieval. This is distinct from basic C-STORE which transfers images — commitment adds the verification step.

front.question: Why is lossy compression not recommended for medical imaging?

back.question: Why is lossy compression not recommended for medical imaging?

options.A: Lossy compression creates files too large for PACS storage
options.B: Lossy compression discards image data during compression, and the original data cannot be perfectly reconstructed — potentially compromising diagnostic quality
options.C: Lossy compression is prohibited under DICOM Part 10 standards
options.D: Lossy compression requires a DICOM conformance statement update from the vendor

back.explanation: Lossy compression sacrifices image quality by discarding data during compression. Unlike lossless compression (JPEG, JPEG 2000, RLE), lossy data cannot be perfectly reconstructed. This is why lossless compression is the standard for diagnostic medical images, and why MQSA explicitly requires mammography images to be uncompressed or losslessly compressed.

front.question: What does GSDF stand for and what is it used for?

back.question: What does GSDF stand for and what is it used for?

options.A: General System Diagnostic Function — used for PACS server performance monitoring
options.B: Grayscale Standard Display Function — the ACR-AAPM standard for matching image intensity levels to monitor brightness so the eye can distinguish contrast
options.C: Graded Scan Display Format — used to encode pixel spacing in DICOM headers
options.D: Global Signal Detection Framework — used to detect motion artifacts in dynamic imaging

back.explanation: GSDF (Grayscale Standard Display Function) is an ACR-AAPM technical standard for electronic practice of medical imaging. Its concept is to match image intensity levels to the monitor's brightness output so the human eye can distinguish contrast differences — critical for accurate diagnosis from diagnostic monitors.

source: lecture: Clinical Engineering / Image Display Quality Control

front.question: For diagnostic monitor display quality control, what is the maximum allowable error tolerance at any luminance level according to the DICOM GSDF standard?

back.question: For diagnostic monitor display quality control, what is the maximum allowable error tolerance at any luminance level according to the DICOM GSDF standard?
back.explanation: The DICOM GSDF display QC standard specifies an error tolerance of 10% at any luminance level. Additional thresholds: maximum brightness >350 cd/m² (>420 cd/m² for mammography), minimum brightness <1 cd/m², and uniformity <15%. Measurements should be taken with a dosimeter under low ambient light.

front.question: Why is the MRI magnet considered always on, even when no patient is being scanned?

back.question: Why is the MRI magnet considered always on, even when no patient is being scanned?

options.A: Hospital policy requires the magnet to remain energized to prevent quenching costs
options.B: The superconducting magnet maintains a constant strong magnetic field and cannot be quickly switched off — ferromagnetic objects are always at risk of being pulled in
options.C: The MRI magnet is only on during scans but IIPs are trained to treat it as always on for safety
options.D: Federal regulations require the magnet to remain powered for radiation monitoring purposes

back.explanation: MRI uses a superconducting magnet that maintains a constant, strong magnetic field — it cannot be quickly switched off like a light. This means ferromagnetic objects (metal tools, devices, laptops) are always at risk of projectile hazards. Clinical staff and IIPs must be aware of MRI safety zones and screened before entering.

front.question: A third-party AI application needs to receive the Modality Worklist from PACS so study instance UIDs (SUIDss) match with the original study. What happens if this is not configured?

back.question: A third-party AI application needs to receive the Modality Worklist from PACS so study instance UIDs (SUIDss) match with the original study. What happens if this is not configured?

options.A: The AI application will refuse to process images until SUID synchronization is completed
options.B: The AI application will generate results that cannot be linked to the correct study in PACS, because DICOM will detect it as a different patient or study
options.C: PACS will automatically assign a new SUID to incoming AI results to prevent conflicts
options.D: The AI results will be stored as a separate patient record with no impact on the original study

back.explanation: If a third-party application (AI, document scanner, OB measurement tool) does not receive the Modality Worklist, its Study Instance UID will not match the original study's SUID. PACS will treat the incoming results as belonging to a different patient or study, preventing correct association. Configuring MWL for all third-party apps is essential for data integrity.

front.question: Radiation therapy integration with the enterprise imaging suite typically requires what?

back.question: Radiation therapy integration with the enterprise imaging suite typically requires what?

options.A: A dedicated nuclear medicine camera installed within the radiation therapy suite
options.B: Integration of third-party treatment planning applications with the VNA via query/retrieve and store functions
options.C: An additional HIPAA compliance officer specifically for radiation oncology records
options.D: MQSA accreditation for all radiation therapy equipment used in treatment planning

back.explanation: Radiation therapy (external beam, brachytherapy, systemic radioisotope) uses third-party treatment planning applications that must integrate with the VNA. These applications need to perform query/retrieve and potentially send results back to PACS/VNA (C-STORE). Tumor tracking applications also integrate with PACS.

source: lecture: Clinical Engineering / 1. Support for Ancillary Services

front.question: A DICOM conformance statement must be made publicly available on the internet. What is the primary purpose of this document?

back.question: A DICOM conformance statement must be publicly available on the internet. What is the primary purpose of this document?

options.A: To certify that the vendor has passed all HIPAA Security Rule requirements
options.B: To describe the system's supported DICOM service classes, transfer syntaxes, information objects, network configuration, limitations, and certifications — enabling integrators to determine how two systems can interoperate
options.C: To document the IIP's qualifications for managing the system
options.D: To provide pricing and licensing information to prospective buyers

back.explanation: A DICOM conformance statement is required to be publicly available and describes everything needed for integration: supported DICOM service classes, transfer syntaxes, information objects, network configuration, limitations, and certifications. IIPs use it to determine whether two systems (e.g., a printer and PACS) can interoperate.

front.question: What is an encapsulated DICOM file type, and what is its primary use case?

back.question: What is an encapsulated DICOM file type, and what is its primary use case?

options.A: A DICOM file with all patient identifiers removed for research use
options.B: A non-DICOM image format (JPEG, PNG, TIFF) wrapped within a DICOM container, typically used for integrating non-radiology ologies into PACS
options.C: A compressed DICOM file stored using lossy JPEG 2000 encoding
options.D: A DICOM file that contains multiple studies combined into a single archive

back.explanation: An encapsulated DICOM file takes a non-DICOM image format (JPEG, PNG, TIFF) and wraps it in a DICOM container using typically lossless compression. This is used for visible light images (endoscopy, dermatology, ophthalmology) and other non-traditional modalities that need to be stored in PACS.

front.question: In the radiology imaging workflow, which portion is governed by DICOM versus HL7?

back.question: In the radiology imaging workflow, which portion is governed by DICOM versus HL7?

options.A: DICOM governs all parts of the workflow; HL7 is only used for billing
options.B: HL7 governs image storage and transfer; DICOM governs order placement and reporting
options.C: DICOM governs image acquisition and reading (scanning and radiologist review); HL7 governs order placement, report distribution, and clinical data exchange
options.D: HL7 and DICOM are interchangeable; either can be used for any part of the workflow

back.explanation: In the radiology workflow, DICOM handles the middle part — scanning the patient (image acquisition) and the radiologist reviewing images. HL7 handles everything else: orders from the ED doctor, report distribution back to the ED and PCP, and all clinical data exchange between EMR, RIS, and other clinical systems.

source: lecture: Imaging Informatics / Medical Imaging Workflows

front.question: The DICOM standard is both a format and a protocol. What does this mean?

back.question: The DICOM standard is both a format and a protocol. What does this mean?

options.A: DICOM defines how images look on screen (format) and how they should be printed (protocol)
options.B: DICOM dictates how data can be stored on disk (.dcm files) AND how DICOM data can be exchanged between systems
options.C: DICOM defines image resolution (format) and network bandwidth requirements (protocol)
options.D: DICOM is a file format created by RSNA and a transmission protocol managed by HL7

back.explanation: DICOM is both a format — defining how files are stored on disk (.dcm files, described in Part 10 of the standard) — and a protocol — defining how DICOM data can be exchanged between systems. DICOM has been on version 3 since the 1990s and is made up of 22 parts.

front.question: What is the correct DICOM data hierarchy from broadest to most specific?

back.question: What is the correct DICOM data hierarchy from broadest to most specific?

options.A: Study → Patient → Series → Instance → Frame
options.B: Patient → Study → Series → Instance → Frame
options.C: Series → Study → Patient → Frame → Instance
options.D: Patient → Instance → Series → Study → Frame

back.explanation: DICOM hierarchy: Patient (pseudo-level containing identifiers) → Study (the imaging exam) → Series (individual view or sequence within the exam) → Instance (individual image object) → Frame (individual image within a multi-frame instance). A chest X-ray study with AP and lateral views = 1 study, 2 series.

front.question: What is DIMSE and how does it differ from DICOMweb?

back.question: What is DIMSE and how does it differ from DICOMweb?

options.A: DIMSE is a web-based RESTful service; DICOMweb is a TCP/IP connection-based protocol
options.B: DIMSE is TCP/IP based with connection-oriented negotiation (chatty); DICOMweb is HTTP-based and RESTful — simpler and stateless
options.C: DIMSE handles report generation; DICOMweb handles image acquisition
options.D: DIMSE is used only for modality worklist; DICOMweb is used only for image storage

back.explanation: DIMSE (DICOM Message Service Element) is TCP/IP based, requiring connection negotiation between SCU and SCP — it is 'chatty' (requires AE recognition, syntax negotiation, then data transfer). DICOMweb is HTTP-based and RESTful — simpler, stateless web calls. Both support query (C-FIND/QIDO-RS), retrieve (C-MOVE/WADO-RS), and store (C-STORE/STOW-RS).

front.question: In DICOMweb, which service is the equivalent of DICOM's traditional C-STORE for image storage?

back.question: In DICOMweb, which service is the equivalent of DICOM's traditional C-STORE for image storage?

options.A: QIDO-RS
options.B: WADO-RS
options.C: STOW-RS
options.D: FHIR ImagingStudy

back.explanation: DICOMweb operation mapping: C-FIND → QIDO-RS (query), C-MOVE/C-GET → WADO-RS (retrieve), C-STORE → STOW-RS (store). STOW-RS is the RESTful web service equivalent of C-STORE for storing DICOM objects via HTTP.

front.question: HL7 v2 is still the most commonly used HL7 standard. What text-based format does it use?

back.question: HL7 v2 is still the most commonly used HL7 standard. What text-based format does it use?

options.A: XML/SOAP
options.B: JSON via HTTP REST
options.C: Hats and Pipes (pipe-delimited segments)
options.D: Fixed-width ASCII columns

back.explanation: HL7 v2 uses a 'hats and pipes' format — pipe-delimited segments that are human-readable but structured. It travels over MLLP (Minimal Lower Layer Protocol). HL7 v3/CDA uses XML/SOAP. HL7 FHIR uses XML, JSON, and others over HTTP REST.

front.question: Which HL7 v2 event type is used for order placement?

back.question: Which HL7 v2 event type is used for order placement?

options.A: ADT (Admit, Discharge, Transfer)
options.B: SIU (Appointment Scheduling)
options.C: ORM (Order placement)
options.D: ORU (Order result updates)

back.explanation: HL7 v2 event types: ADT = Admit/Discharge/Transfer (patient movement), SIU = Scheduling information unsolicited (appointment scheduling), ORM = Order messages (order placement), ORU = Observation result unsolicited (order result/report updates). Knowing these event types is essential for troubleshooting HL7 integrations.

front.question: HL7 FHIR was released in 2014. Which version do US production EMRs currently speak?

back.question: HL7 FHIR was released in 2014. Which version do US production EMRs currently speak?

options.A: Release 2 (R2)
options.B: Release 3 (R3)
options.C: Release 4 (R4)
options.D: Release 5 (R5)

back.explanation: While the latest FHIR version is Release 5 (R5), US EMRs in production currently speak FHIR Release 4 (R4). When building integrations with live production EMRs in the US, R4 is the target. R5 may be used for new development work that doesn't require immediate production EMR compatibility.

source: lecture: Imaging Informatics / Integrations of Systems

front.question: What does IHE stand for and who sponsors it?

back.question: What does IHE stand for and who sponsors it?

options.A: International Health Exchange, sponsored by WHO and HL7
options.B: Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise, sponsored by HIMSS and RSNA
options.C: Imaging Healthcare Engineering, sponsored by ACR and SIIM
options.D: Interoperability in Health Environments, sponsored by the Joint Commission

back.explanation: IHE stands for Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise. It is a nonprofit body sponsored by HIMSS (Healthcare Information and Management Systems Society) and RSNA (Radiological Society of North America). IHE creates integration 'profiles' built on top of standards like DICOM, HL7, and FHIR.

front.question: The Scheduled Workflow (SWF) is often called the 'mother of all profiles.' What does it define?

back.question: The Scheduled Workflow (SWF) is often called the 'mother of all profiles.' What does it define?

options.A: How audit logs are generated and stored for HIPAA compliance
options.B: How images are consistently rendered across different display workstations
options.C: How the radiology workflow works — defining all the systems involved and how they use DICOM and HL7 to exchange information through the entire imaging process
options.D: How patient information is reconciled when moving between institutions

back.explanation: SWF (Scheduled Workflow) is the foundational IHE Radiology profile that defines the entire radiology workflow — from order to image acquisition to report. It specifies the actors (EMR, RIS, modality, PACS, etc.) and the transactions between them using DICOM and HL7. ATNA covers audit trails; CPI covers image rendering; PIR covers patient reconciliation.

source: lecture: Imaging Informatics / IHE Integration Profiles

front.question: The IHE IOCM profile addresses a common enterprise imaging challenge. What problem does it solve?

back.question: The IHE IOCM profile addresses a common enterprise imaging challenge. What problem does it solve?

options.A: It enables AI applications to prioritize the radiologist reading worklist
options.B: It defines how DICOM studies that have flowed to multiple systems (e.g., PACS and VNA) can be synchronized after a change such as a patient demographics update
options.C: It governs how mammography images must be printed to comply with MQSA
options.D: It enables radiologists to embed hyperlinks to specific images directly within their reports

back.explanation: IOCM (Imaging Object Change Management) defines how DICOM studies distributed across multiple systems (e.g., PACS and VNA) can be synchronized after changes such as patient demographics corrections. Without IOCM, a study corrected in PACS may remain incorrect in VNA or vice versa.

source: lecture: Imaging Informatics / IHE Integration Profiles

front.question: Which new IHE profile enables interactive radiology reports with embedded hyperlinks that jump directly to specific images?

back.question: Which new IHE profile enables interactive radiology reports with embedded hyperlinks that jump directly to specific images?

options.A: AIW (AI Workflow)
options.B: POWR (Prioritization of Worklists for Reporting)
options.C: IMR (Interactive Multimedia Reporting)
options.D: IRA (Integrated Real-time Applications)

back.explanation: IMR (Interactive Multimedia Reporting) allows reports to become interactive by embedding hyperlinks — e.g., a link to a specific image slice (such as 'nodule on slice 159'). Referring physicians can click the link and jump directly to that image. IRA handles real-time data exchange; POWR handles worklist prioritization; AIW handles AI workflow.

source: lecture: Imaging Informatics / IHE Integration Profiles

front.question: Dermatology imaging differs from radiology in a way that creates a unique integration challenge for enterprise imaging. What is it?

back.question: Dermatology imaging differs from radiology in a way that creates a unique integration challenge for enterprise imaging. What is it?

options.A: Dermatology images are too large for standard PACS storage
options.B: Dermatology images use a proprietary format incompatible with DICOM
options.C: Dermatology imaging is encounter-based — photos are taken without formal orders — which means standard order-driven PACS workflows do not apply
options.D: Dermatology requires a separate HIPAA compliance officer for each imaging session

back.explanation: Dermatology imaging is encounter-based — images are captured without formal orders created in the EMR. This breaks the standard DICOM Modality Worklist-driven workflow (no order = no accession number = no MWL entry). Enterprise imaging architectures must accommodate order-less acquisition workflows for specialties like dermatology.

front.question: What are the typical systems involved in a medical imaging architecture?

back.question: What are the typical systems involved in a medical imaging architecture?

options.A: Only PACS and modalities
options.B: EMR (with RIS module), interface engine & routers, image manager (PACS/VNA), PACS clients/enterprise viewers, and modalities & other capture devices
options.C: DICOM router, VNA, HL7 broker, and radiology reporting system
options.D: EMR, LOINC server, SNOMED database, and PACS printer

back.explanation: The typical systems in a medical imaging architecture are: EMR (with RIS module), interface engine & routers, image manager (PACS and/or VNA), PACS clients/enterprise viewers (including zero-footprint viewers), and modalities and other capture devices (including non-traditional devices for enterprise imaging).

front.question: FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) introduced which key architectural change compared to HL7 v2?

back.question: FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) introduced which key architectural change compared to HL7 v2?

options.A: FHIR uses MLLP transport instead of HTTP for improved security
options.B: FHIR is resource-based and uses a pull-and-push model over HTTP REST, whereas HL7 v2 uses pipe-delimited messages over MLLP
options.C: FHIR eliminated the need for conformance statements because all resources are pre-validated
options.D: FHIR requires a dedicated server running on port 2575, while HL7 v2 uses port 443

back.explanation: HL7 FHIR (released 2014) is resource-based (Patient, Encounter, ImagingStudy, DiagnosticReport, etc.), uses HTTP REST, supports XML/JSON and more (syntax agnostic), and supports pull and push models. HL7 v2 uses pipe-delimited 'hats and pipes' format over MLLP — a fundamentally different architecture.

front.question: An IIP needs to confirm whether two DICOM systems can interoperate before starting an integration project. What is the first document they should obtain from each vendor?

back.question: An IIP needs to confirm whether two DICOM systems can interoperate before starting an integration project. What is the first document they should obtain from each vendor?

options.A: The vendor's HIPAA Business Associate Agreement (BAA)
options.B: The DICOM Conformance Statement — publicly available from each vendor, describing supported service classes, transfer syntaxes, and information objects
options.C: The vendor's IHE Connectathon test results from the most recent event
options.D: The system's HL7 v2 integration manual, which covers both DICOM and HL7 services

back.explanation: Before integrating two DICOM systems, obtain the DICOM Conformance Statement from each vendor. These publicly available documents describe each system's supported DICOM service classes, transfer syntaxes, and information objects. Comparing the two statements reveals whether they share compatible capabilities.

source: lecture: Imaging Informatics / Integrations of Systems

front.question: A radiology system needs to allow DICOM queries for specific studies AND allow clients to retrieve the located images. Which pair of traditional DICOM operations handles these two tasks?

back.question: A radiology system needs to allow DICOM queries for specific studies AND allow clients to retrieve the located images. Which pair of traditional DICOM operations handles these two tasks?

options.A: C-STORE for query; C-FIND for retrieval
options.B: C-FIND for query; C-MOVE or C-GET for retrieval
options.C: QIDO-RS for query; STOW-RS for retrieval
options.D: C-MOVE for query; C-STORE for retrieval

back.explanation: In traditional DICOM (DIMSE): C-FIND is used for query (find studies matching criteria). C-MOVE allows the server to push images to a specified destination; C-GET allows the client to pull images directly. In DICOMweb, the equivalents are QIDO-RS (query) and WADO-RS (retrieve). C-STORE is for storage/sending, not retrieval.

front.question: The IHE REM profile is used for what purpose in imaging informatics?

back.question: The IHE REM profile is used for what purpose in imaging informatics?

options.A: Reconciling patient information when studies are transferred between institutions
options.B: Reporting and recording ionizing radiation exposure received by patients during imaging exams
options.C: Managing access controls for reading workstation remote connections
options.D: Providing portable imaging data on CDs and USB drives

back.explanation: REM (Radiation Exposure Monitoring) is the IHE profile for reporting and recording ionizing radiation that a patient received during an imaging exam. This supports dose tracking and patient safety monitoring. PDI (Portable Data for Imaging) handles CDs/USBs; PIR handles patient reconciliation.

source: lecture: Imaging Informatics / IHE Integration Profiles

front.question: Which FHIR-related extension allows clinical decision support (CDS) services to run as hooks within an EHR workflow?

back.question: Which FHIR-related extension allows clinical decision support (CDS) services to run as hooks within an EHR workflow?

options.A: FHIRcast
options.B: CDS Hooks
options.C: SMART on FHIR
options.D: QIDO-RS

back.explanation: CDS Hooks is a FHIR-related framework that allows external clinical decision support services to integrate into EHR workflows as 'hooks' — triggering at specific workflow events. FHIRcast enables real-time context synchronization between applications. SMART on FHIR enables secure app authorization against FHIR APIs.

front.question: A pathology department wants to join the enterprise imaging system. Their digital slides average 500 MB each. The surgery department also wants to store operative recordings averaging 4 GB each. What critical infrastructure consideration does this raise?

back.question: A pathology department wants to join the enterprise imaging system. Their digital slides average 500 MB each. The surgery department also wants to store operative recordings averaging 4 GB each. What critical infrastructure consideration does this raise?

options.A: These file types require a dedicated HIPAA compliance officer for each specialty
options.B: These specialties require significantly larger storage capacity than traditional radiology; the VNA or PACS storage architecture must be re-evaluated and scaled to accommodate the volume and growth
options.C: Pathology and surgery images must be stored in a separate PACS because DICOM does not support non-radiology specialties
options.D: These departments need separate HL7 v2 interfaces because they use different message types than radiology

back.explanation: Enterprise imaging expansion brings dramatically larger file sizes: digital pathology ~2–3 GB, surgical videos ~4 GB, compared to CT exams at ~30 MB. This dwarfs traditional radiology volumes and requires IIPs to re-evaluate and scale storage architecture (VNA capacity, I/O throughput, tiered storage) before onboarding these specialties.

front.question: DICOM Part 10 describes what specific aspect of the DICOM standard?

back.question: DICOM Part 10 describes what specific aspect of the DICOM standard?

options.A: The RESTful DICOMweb services (QIDO-RS, WADO-RS, STOW-RS)
options.B: How DICOM files are persisted on disk as .dcm files
options.C: The radiology workflow integration profile for scheduled imaging
options.D: The DICOM security and authentication services

back.explanation: DICOM Part 10 describes how DICOM files can be persisted onto disk using the .dcm file format. DICOMweb services are described in Part 18. The 22-part DICOM standard covers different aspects of format, protocol, services, and security across its parts.

source: lecture: Imaging Informatics / Integrations of Systems

front.question: What is the primary purpose of IHE Connectathons?

back.question: What is the primary purpose of IHE Connectathons?

options.A: Annual conferences where IHE releases new integration profile specifications
options.B: Week-long events where vendors claiming to support IHE profiles test their integrations against other vendors' systems in a live test environment
options.C: Certification exams for IIPs to demonstrate knowledge of IHE profiles
options.D: Government-mandated audits to verify healthcare organizations comply with IHE standards

back.explanation: IHE Connectathons are week-long events where vendors who claim to support IHE integration profiles get together to test their integrations nonstop — verifying that their implementations truly interoperate as the profiles specify. This real-world testing is a core mechanism for validating profile implementations.

source: lecture: Imaging Informatics / IHE Integration Profiles
